Le metriche che le piattaforme sono tenute a comunicare soddisfano solo gli indicatori di realizzazione. 

Se le piattaforme, pertanto, non garantiscono l’accesso alle serie di dati la Commissione non potrà 

verificare le informazioni trasmesse. Di conseguenza, il codice non realizza il proprio obiettivo di far sì 

che le piattaforme online rendano conto delle proprie azioni e del ruolo svolto nella lotta attiva alla 

disinformazione ma conferisce dei voti sulla base di dati univoci e non cumulativamente paragonabili.  

Appare significativo infatti che la Commissione europea, in relazione all'assenza di indicatori chiave di 

performance (ICP o KPI) globali per tutti i firmatari, abbia rilevato la difficoltà di individuare ICP in 

grado di monitorare le azioni di tutti i firmatari del codice di buone pratiche, data la natura e le attività 

molto diverse dei firmatari e i diversi livelli di impegno, ad esempio, i servizi forniti dalle piattaforme dei 

social media variano notevolmente dai servizi forniti dai motori di ricerca32. 

 

5. Le azioni disinformanti e i rischi per lo Stato di una valutazione con KPI 

L’importanza di contrastare i fenomeni di disordine informativo (di cui la disinformazione “pura” è solo 

una delle condotte più riconoscibili) è quella di evitare che tali azioni, facenti parte dello strumentario 

delle guerre ibride33, rischino di compromettere tanto i diritti dei singoli cittadini quanto l’integrità stessa 

degli Stati34. 

Il fenomeno del disordine informativo può sussumere al suo interno diverse condotte, a volte non 

necessariamente volontarie e dolose35 che possono essere sussunte nelle cinque macro categorie di:  

 disinformazione,  

 misinformazione,  

 informazione malevola, 

 malinformazione, 

 informazione improvvida o coperta da recentismo (midinformation). 

Nel caso della prima tipologia, ovvero della disinformazione “pura” o “classica”, si tratta di una condotta 

che prevede la volontaria creazione e/o diffusione dolosa di informazioni false, allo scopo di arrecare 

danni ad uno Stato o ad un sistema di Paesi, con canali informativi/mediatici integrati o congiuntamente 

facilmente influenzabili (in quest’ultimo caso, si può considerare che il risultato di una campagna riuscita

di disinformazione verso, ad esempio, la Francia può originare una misinformazione in Italia o viceversa). 

Fino alla prima metà del XX sec. le operazioni di disinformazione sono state appannaggio quasi esclusivo 

degli apparati nazionali di informazione e sicurezza, che le adottavano per influenzare pro domo loro gli 

Stati satelliti o per “preparare il terreno” ad eventuali azioni belliche in Paesi ostili. Con l’evoluzione dei 

mezzi di comunicazione di massa (la diffusione, per scopi civili, del canale di Internet) e la comparsa di 

attori globali non nazionali e asimmetrici si sono sviluppate anche azioni di disinformazione trasversali e 

non nazionali, che possono perseguire fini criminali “terzi” e contrari rispetto alle politiche degli Stati o 

agire in loro appoggio ma senza dipenderne direttamente, in modo da mascherare gli obiettivi e di 

mimetizzarsi tra forme di informazione d’inchiesta e/o filantropiche.  

Un’altra tecnica di disinformazione è la propaganda disinformante36, che più che non tende solo alla 

pubblicizzazione di notizie edulcorate per influenzare l’opinione pubblica interna ma ha in quest’ultima 

il canale (e non l’obiettivo) di diffusione della falsa notizia, secondo il principio “se ci credono i nostri 

cittadini ci crederà anche il nemico”. 

La misinformazione, invece, consiste in una condotta che prevede la creazione e/o diffusione 

inconsapevole di informazioni false. In questo caso colui che diffonde o genera la notizia (magari, ad 

esempio, dopo aver visionato una fonte audio-visiva o traducendo la stampa estera) è in buona fede e 

pensa di rendere un servizio alla collettività, senza accorgersi che, invece, sta contribuendo a propagare 

disinformazione. Si tratta, per questo, di un’azione che non comporta il dolo da parte degli attori principali 

(giornalisti, membri delle Istituzioni o informatori a vario titolo) ma che integra, a seconda delle 

fattispecie, le possibili responsabilità colpose per imperizia, culpa in vigilando e scarsa professionalità. 

Oltre agli strumenti “classici” per indurre la misinformazione (ad es. voci diffuse ad arte, false fonti di 

notizia fatte trovare ad improvvidi funzionari o giornalisti o personaggi mediaticamente influenti) e alla 

misinformazione indotta “di riflesso” per disinformazione altrui, grazie all’affinamento delle tecniche 

digitali di “effetti speciali” è nato -nell’ultimo decennio- lo strumento del deep-fake37, che consiste 

nell’alterazione, per rielaborazione informatica artificiale, di un contenuto audiovisivo, con un grado di 

verosimiglianza tale da essere difficilmente distinguibile dal vero per l’occhio umano. La misinformazione 

aumenta la propria efficacia in proporzione al grado di fama e affidabilità della fonte che, 

inconsapevolmente, la crea (tanto sarà noto e popolare l’autore della notizia tanto risulterà attendibile il 

contenuto della stessa; secondo il principio per cui “se lo dice lui/lei sarà vero”).

L’informazione malevola, da malicious information (“informazione dannosa” o “i. maliziosa”), a sua volta, 

consiste nella diffusione volontaria e dolosa di notizie vere ma coperte da un regime di riservatezza (in 

Italia se ne distinguono quattro livelli: riservato “R”, riservatissimo “RR”, segreto “S” e segretissimo 

“SS”), allo scopo di creare conseguenze avverse e/o scredito nelle Istituzioni (principalmente governi) 

che le hanno segretate. Molto spesso tale condotta è perpetrata a seguito di altri atti di guerra ibrida come 

attacchi hacker ai server governativi e/o banche dati riservate, sottrazione materiale di documenti di 

importanza strategica o corruzione di funzionari preposti.  Quest’ultima non deve essere confusa con la 

malinformazione che è un'informazione accurata ma diffusa con intento malevolo. Si tratta di materiale 

sensibile che viene diffuso per danneggiare qualcuno o la sua reputazione. Tra gli esempi vi sono il doxing, 

il revenge porn e l'editing di video per rimuovere contesti o contenuti importanti.38  

Infine, l’informazione improvvida o influenzata da recentismo (in inglese definita anche midinformation) è 

una quarta categoria che contribuisce ad incrementare il disordine informativo, che personalmente 

ritengo esser ben distinta dalle precedenti, è rappresentata dalla diffusione avventata di notizie (c.d. 

informazioni improvvide o influenzate da recentismo) vere ma parziali o non ancora completamente 

definite, rispetto ad avvenimenti complessi e molto recenti.39 Con l’affermazione delle piattaforme social 

e, in generale, del Web trai canali di diffusione delle notizie e di contenuti audiovisivi, il mondo 

dell’informazione è stato progressivamente influenzato, tanto nella metodica della ricerca delle fonti 

quanto nelle tempistiche narrative, tendendo a privilegiare sempre più la narrazione mediatica ed 

emozionale dei fatti rispetto al loro approfondimento critico contenutistico. La “corsa all’esclusiva” 

(ovvero a fornire per primi un’informazione per superare la concorrenza mediatica) origina, in contesti 

complessi e mutevoli (come nel caso dell’emergenza pandemica o degli eventi bellici d’Ucraina), 

informazioni parziali o contraddittorie con gli sviluppi successivi dei fatti o con gli stessi approfondimenti 

tematici, generando confusione e disorientamento nell’opinione pubblica, nonché senso di sfiducia verso 

le istituzioni e i canali d’informazione accreditati, lasciando così spazio al possibile insinuarsi di eventuali 

azioni di disinformazione o misinformazione indotta.  

Per quanto azioni tecnicamente dissimili, considerata la poliedricità delle tecnologie di canale mediatico 

e l’interconnessione dei rapporti causa-effetto, spesso si possono riscontrare contemporaneamente le 

quattro condotte disinformanti, direttamente concatenate tra loro40.

Considerata l’estrema versatilità delle condotte di disordine informativo (sopradescritte) e la sottigliezza 

con le quali le stesse si possono combinare tra loro – per occultarsi, non essere rilevate e centrare così 

l’obiettivo – appare evidente come tutti i fenomeni disinformanti siano considerati un rischio per gli Stati, 

al centro delle agende internazionali di prevenzione e contrasto41.  

I rischi per lo Stato posso essere di diversa natura, andando da aspetti marginali e connessi solo alla mera 

informazione dei singoli cittadini (comunque sancita dal diritto costituzionale ad essere informati, ex art. 

21 Cost.) alla destabilizzazione politica e sociale dell’intero Paese colpito42.  

La limitazione del diritto all’informazione generale, la manipolazione dell’opinione pubblica da parte di 

Paesi ostili, lo scredito sistematico delle Istituzioni, l’infiltrazione nel circuito dell’informazione e della 

sicurezza pubblica, la destabilizzazione politico-sociale, la compromissione, il furto e il danneggiamento 

dei dati veicolati nei circuiti informativi portanti e degli stessi sistemi di archiviazione e diffusione, sono 

solo i principali rischi – posti in un’evidente scala crescente – che lo Stato può correre se non contrasta 

efficacemente le campagne disinformanti43. 

Nel caso di un’unica valutazione ex post dell’idoneità delle piattaforme e degli operatori del mondo di 

Internet al contrasto della disinformazione, tali rischi potrebbero anche aumentare, in quanto l’uso degli 

strumenti numerici non fornisce alcun ausilio nel bloccare o nel riconoscere le condotte di disordine 

informativo ex ante o in fieri ma “premia” o “punisce”, a seconda di minacce tracciate dai soggetti 

interessati con criteri totalmente autonomi, singolari e non trasparenti. 

Gli indicatori chiave di prestazione (o KPI), utilizzati secondo i criteri quantitativi (degli SLI) o qualitativi 

(dei QRE) non potranno fornire nemmeno una completa ed affidabile valutazione posteriore dell’operato 

dei soggetti monitorati dalla Commissione UE, ai sensi del DSA.  

Non sarà possibile, infatti, valutare quanti dei tentativi di disinformazione sono “andati a bersaglio”, 

poiché il criterio quantitativo degli SLI si basa sui dati complessivi delle minacce rilevate dalla piattaforma 

e, pertanto, efficacemente bloccati ma non calcola – ovviamente – quelle azioni che, non essendo rilevate, 

                                                           
sottratte e desegretate) aumentando così il disordine informativo. Alcuni comparti della libera informazione (sia nazionali 
che esteri) riprendono quest’ultime notizie, presentandole come verità e accreditandole con la loro attendibilità di 
testata/autore, diffondendole ulteriormente.  
Come si potrà notare nell’esempio, costruito ad hoc (ma, comunque, riconducibile a specifici casi verificatisi), si 
intrecciano, concatenandosi, tutte le quattro forme di disordine informativo. Da una situazione iniziale di notizie affette 
da recentismo e non accuratamente accertate, si passa alla possibile disinformazione e malinformazione operata da una 
Potenza ostile, che se non opportunamente rilevata e contrastata (nel mondo di Internet diventa più difficile perché le 
notizie permangono e, come in un’Idra, si moltiplicano in svariati canali/forme) si estenderà ulteriormente 
ammantandosi di attendibilità grazie ai media che la diffondono.

hanno immesso informazioni malevole o false nel sistema a danno degli utenti. Non può stabilire così 

l’efficacia stessa della quantità delle azioni intraprese, quindi dei risultati in cui il contrasto è stato efficace, 

non avendo cognizione del numero totale dei tentativi (positivi e negativi) di disinformazione a cui è stata 

sottoposta la piattaforma. 

Allo stesso modo e anche in parte per il motivo suddetto, è totalmente relativo e irrilevante ai fini del 

report generale e del progressivo miglioramento della prevenzione anti-disinformazione il dato misurato 

dall’indicatore numerico della qualità delle azioni di contrasto intraprese (QRE). Il parametro di scala, 

oltre a variare di piattaforma in piattaforma, non può essere considerato soddisfacente in quanto misura 

solo una precisa azione di contrasto che per un soggetto monitorato può essere qualitativamente elevato 

o meno ma solo in reazione alle sue capacità di contrasto e alla natura stessa della minaccia, che comunque 

è solo quella già quantitativamente rilevata e che, per questo, non può misurare la qualità negativa delle 

disinformazioni andate a segno.  

L’uso di indicatori numerici per valutare le condotte di contrasto di un fenomeno, così sfaccettato e 

multiforme, come il disordine informativo rischia, inoltre, di sminuire sensibilmente la natura stessa della 

minaccia e dei “beni” tutelati. Il godimento dei diritti costituzionali relativi all’informazione e alla 

manifestazione del pensiero, garantiti dall’ampio ventaglio di applicazione dell’art. 21 Cost. e dalle leggi a 

cui sono soggetti i mas media e le loro pagine di canalizzazione ufficiale via Internet, sarebbe 

eventualmente limitato e banalizzato a una valutazione numerica ex post su dati forniti dagli stessi soggetti 

controllati (nella maggior parte dei casi, relativi a grandi imprese del digitale non appartenenti all’Unione 

Europea).  

Sarebbe molto arduo definire, in base solo agli indicatori di valutazione ex post, tanto quantitativi che 

qualitativi, se una piattaforma, nel contrastare le notizie false, ha rispettato anche i diritti del singolo di 

esprimere le proprie opinioni (quindi manifestare il proprio pensiero) per quanto fantasiose e irrealistiche 

queste possano essere o ha qualificato le stesse come fake news, censurandole e annoverandole trai tentativi 

riusciti (secondo parametro quantitativo – SLI) di contrasto.  

L’inevitabile competizione commerciale tra gli operatori di Internet tenderebbe a spingere i soggetti 

valutati, con gli indicatori numerici qualitativi e quantitativi, a non curarsi molto della qualità dell’azione 

di contrasto ma del numero della stessa, in base esclusivamente dei risultati numerici “aleatori” da 

raggiungere. Per tutto questo, una valutazione basata esclusivamente su base numerica, come nel caso di 

un algoritmo, potrebbe mostrare delle serie difficoltà a comprendere un portato di valori e un delicato 

bilanciamento degli stessi su cui si fondano gli ordinamenti costituzionali dello Stato di diritto 

contemporaneo44.

6. Conclusioni e possibili integrazioni migliorative dell’uso degli indicatori numerici 

Dal punto di vista del diritto costituzionale (e non solo) appare evidente come sia i Service Level Indicators 

(SLI) che i Qualitative Reporting Elements (QRE) non possano essere l’unico parametro per valutare, ai sensi 

del Digital Service Act e del Codice rafforzato di buone pratiche sulla disinformazione, il contrasto del 

disordine informativo attuato dalle piattaforme digitali, operanti nel mercato UE. 

Sarebbe riduttivo pensare che una valutazione ex post, che fotografa solo dati forniti – secondo criteri 

d’intervento autonomi e difformi – dai soggetti controllati sia sufficiente a certificare l’assoluta sicurezza 

di certe imprese digitali rispetto alla moltitudine di condotte disinformanti. Sarebbe, altresì, illusorio 

reputare che un tale sistema di valutazione numerica spingerebbe, per il principio della libera concorrenza 

nel mercato, ad un miglioramento virtuoso dei soggetti giudicati e, quindi, ad una loro maggiore 

attenzione – non solo a prevenire e contrastare le condotte digitali malevole – ma anche a tutelare diversi 

diritti costituzionali (compresi quelli alla rispettabilità, alla reputazione e alla riservatezza) fondati 

(positivamente o negativamente45) sul diritto all’informazione. 

Allo stesso modo, sarebbe erroneo pensare di utilizzare gli indicatori numerici come gli unici parametri 

valutativi per giudicare la sicurezza del mercato unico digitale europeo e, pertanto, la tenuta stessa dei 

singoli Stati membri davanti alla minaccia delle condotte disinformanti che, per loro stessa molteplicità e 

accuratezza, possono sfuggire a notevoli controlli. Lasciando che ciascuna piattaforma, sotto valutazione, 

proceda a suo piacimento ad identificarli o meno (visto che solo i dati finali trasmessi, dalla stessa, saranno 

oggetto di controllo qualitativo e quantitativo) non solo si crea un’evidente disparità tra i soggetti giudicati 

ma non si impone nemmeno un criterio unico (per quanto sia arduo) con cui i controlli ex ante debbano 

essere fatti. 

Cercando un primo risconto concreto dell’efficacia dei KPI del Codice rafforzato, pur non essendo in 

presenza di molti dati trasversali da poter incrociare per confutazione e confronto, si segnala uno studio 

pubblicato, nell’estate 2024, dal sito europeo Internet Policy Review46. Tale documento che analizza per 

la prima volta la conformità di Google, Meta, Microsoft, TikTok e Twitter (ora X) ai loro obblighi di 

rendicontazione si nota come i rapporti di riferimento delle piattaforme pubblicati all'inizio del 2023, 

mostrino, nel complesso, che le piattaforme sono solo parzialmente conformi al Codice. Le informazioni 

qualitative fornite dalle piattaforme spesso non sono dettagliate e/o rilevanti. I dati quantitativi riportati 

sono, in molti casi, mancanti, incompleti o non solidi. Si segnala, inoltre, che le attestazioni delle

dal titolare o custodita adeguatamente (nella macro-area del diritto alla privacy).

piattaforme sono in molti casi dubbie o addirittura errate, e viene evidenziata le possibilità di ricerca e di

Certamente tale report, non è dirimente nel definire la totale mancata efficacia dei KPI, che anzi hanno il

delle piattaforme ma solo un primo e parziale segnale che ancora bisognerà migliorare il funzionamento

Un eventuale uso di parametri numerici e, più complessamente algoritmici, nell’identificare e nel

quantitativi) che possano aiutare il controllo dei soggetti preposti ad intercettare quelle forme di 

tecnologia, sempre più evolute, che sfuggono all’occhio umano; secondo il principio che un numero 

scompone o raddoppia, quindi confuta, un altro numero.

The quantification of behavioural forecast in 

administrative legal practice: 

the algorithmic risk assessment of the ETIAS* 

 

di David San-Martín Segura 

Lecturer in Administrative Law 

Universidad de la Rioja 

 

Abstract [En]: The European Travel Information and Authorisation System (ETIAS) is a pre-entry administrative 
system for the management of applications for access to the Schengen area. Its algorithmic profiling device is a 
case of behavioural risk assessment for administrative police decision-making by automated means. The paper 
critically analyses this legal-technological regime considering its functionality, regulatory features and epistemic 
implications. 
 
Titolo: La quantificazione delle previsioni comportamentali nella pratica giuridica amministrativa: la valutazione 
del rischio algoritmico dell’ETIAS 
Abstract [It]: Il Sistema europeo di informazione e autorizzazione ai viaggi (ETIAS) è un sistema amministrativo 
di pre-ingresso per la gestione delle domande di accesso allo spazio Schengen. Il suo dispositivo di profilazione 
algoritmica è un caso di valutazione del rischio comportamentale per il processo decisionale della polizia 
amministrativa con mezzi automatizzati. Il documento analizza criticamente questo regime giuridico-tecnologico 
considerandone la sua funzionalità, la sua costruzione normativa e le sue implicazioni epistemiche. 
 
Keywords: ETIAS; risk profiling; algorithmic decision-making; border control management; Schengen area 
Parole chiave: ETIAS; profilazione del rischio; processo decisionale algoritmico; gestione dei controlli di 
frontiera; spazio Schengen 
 
Table of contents: 1. Introduction. 2. Border management as a risk space. 3. The architecture of the ETIAS. 3.1. 
Organisational structure and administrative-technological procedure. 3.2. The profiling engine: screening rules and 
risk indicators. 4. A blank technological regulation. 4.1. Substantive legality issues. 4.2. Frontex empowerment and 
its limits. 5. The exercise of public powers by algorithmic means at the European level. 5.1. ETIAS as automated 
data processing. 5.2. A high-risk AI system? 6. Epistemic concerns. 6.1. The predictive quantification of behaviour. 
6.2. Effective remedies to algorithmic decision-making. 7. Concluding remarks.  
 

1. Introduction 

The concept of profiling legally qualifies a type of technological mediation: the automated processing of 

data for the purpose of evaluating personal aspects of natural persons. This has been defined, at least 

sectorally, by the regulation of personal data protection, which identifies this type of processing to 

«analyse or predict» aspects relating to the professional performance, economic situation, health, personal

Derecho» (PID2022-136548NB-I00) and «Historia y presente del control social, las instituciones punitivas y los cuerpos 
de seguridad en España (siglos XX-XXI): prácticas, discursos y representaciones culturales» (PID2021-123504NB-I00).

             |n. 3/2025 

 

 

 

 
 

 

preferences, interests, reliability, behaviour, location or movements of the individuals associated with the 

profile [Art. 4(4) GDPR]. 

These are typically predictive practices developed by algorithmic means that can have varying degrees of 

abstraction and opacity and can be based on different volumes of data. Although their use has become 

widespread in commercial practices (for personalised marketing and profiled service offers) and private 

legal field (credit assessment and personnel selection, among others),1 there are growing applications in 

public law for, among other things, planning inspection activities, guiding police practices, adopting 

preventive procedural measures and issuing legal decisions based on behavioural predictions (for 

example, of a penitentiary or even criminal judicial nature).2 They are quantitative models of behavioural 

assessment that, in an epistemic sense, affirm the capacity to analyse human behaviour in probabilistic 

terms, no longer simply to expose trends at an aggregate level but to offer individualised predictions and 

to determine legal decisions on individual cases. Profiling is expressed in such cases as behavioural risk 

assessment. 

In EU law, border management in the Schengen area is a significant case of this type of technological 

irruption. Regulation (EU) 2018/1240 created the «European Travel Information and Authorisation 

System» (ETIAS),3 an administrative mechanism for processing applications for visa-exempt access to 

the European area for third-country nationals (TCNs). In this procedure, the administrative authorisation 

is linked to an assessment of the risks associated with the applicants’ entry into the European area. An 

assessment of the reliability and potential behaviour of the persons concerned is carried out automatically 

by means of the algorithm announced in the Regulation. 

The trend towards quantification and automation of behavioural prognoses has been studied mainly in 

the field of criminal law. The redefinition of the classic category of dangerousness in terms of risk 

management has been observed as an expression of overtly managerial government postulates, focused

on the efficiency of punitive practices4. The case of the ETIAS, on the other hand, illustrates the extent 

of these transformations in administrative policing practices that are not linked (at least predominantly) 

to criminal investigations. The development of data processing capacities and their algorithmic modelling 

have cross-cutting impacts on public law, possibly greater in legal contexts more flexible in the face of 

managerial demands. The administrative management of border control is presented as an area of 

governance specifically linked to the logic of risk and, also, to its evolution as algorithmic risk. 

The analysis of the ETIAS profiling device can only be prospective, as the development of the 

technological model is still in progress5. Nevertheless, the Regulation (which is formally in force) enables 

some reflections on the algorithmic-legal model it constructs. In contrast to de facto adoption of 

algorithmic systems in the public sector, the ETIAS Regulation expresses an attempt at the regulatory 

construction of the automated profiling model by guiding the stages in the development of the risk 

detection system. In this sense, the ETIAS can be seen as a laboratory of algorithmic regulation, which 

has in fact been extrapolated to the Visa Information System (VIS)6. If migration and border management 

is a common area of technological experimentation7, the ETIAS itself is a test for the use of algorithmic 

profiling in this administrative context8. 

The regulatory effort is, however, problematic. On the one hand, attention must be paid to the way in 

which the Regulation conceives the legal meaning of the technological model and concretises it in an 

effective way. On the other hand, the centrality of the algorithmic detection model in the Regulation, and 

how this affects fundamental requirements of administrative law (the position of the affected subjects, 

the rule of law, due process) should be scrutinized. Border and migration management in the EU Area 

of Freedom, Security and Justice (AFSJ) shows a drift towards technological solutionism under the 

imperatives of data-driven management and interoperability9. This algorithmic grafting, Penasa says,

I propose an approach to the ETIAS Regulation in this light by first explaining how it fits into the general 

framework of border management generated in the Schengen area (§2). I then offer a summary

this regime fits into European law both on data protection and, perhaps, on AI (§5). Finally, I suggest 

some critical reflections on the epistemic foundations underpinning this type of predictive quantification 

of behaviour, its algorithmic expression and the legal alterations it brings about (§6). 

 

2. Border management as a risk space 

The algorithmic system implemented by the ETIAS must be understood in the institutional context of 

border management, a sphere of government that is distinctly preventive and instrumental. It seeks to 

manage «mobility» with a logic of selection, categorisation or classification, discriminating functional or 

acceptable mobility (essential in a globalised social and economic order) from that which is dysfunctional 

because of threats of various kinds12. The border is a space of acceptance/rejection decision-making that 

faces a managerial dilemma13: to fulfil its classificatory routine without hindering the functional mobility

the New Pact on Migration and Asylum, focused on the classification of TCNs and the expansion of

«Excess mobility» is understood as risk. It is a collateral, persistent consequence of freedom of movement 

as a functional ingredient and condition of the internal European space. The administrative mechanism 

of the ETIAS is therefore inscribed in an area that is profoundly influenced by the logic of risk. 

It is a management context that also relies on broad technological support from the «smart borders 

solution» or «high-tech border approach», adopted at the beginning of the 2010s17. The management of 

border risk has found its place in the implementation of normative technologies that have brought about 

a sort of spatial and temporal virtualisation of the border18. The aim is to build an «‘ecosystem’ of 

information management capabilities» consisting of a network of interoperable nodes19. In the words of 

Musco Eklund, the border becomes a technological risk filter20. 

Technological infrastructures have played a central role in the European policy on the AFSJ. It is a vector 

of European integration in a field traditionally reserved to the sovereignty of the member states and to 

inter-state cooperation. The technological framework in the field of security and border control provides 

a kind of European digital sovereignty21. On the other hand, the technological solution has both an

interviews as migration management tools and, even more specifically, their adequacy, relevance and 

necessity for the purpose of risk analysis inherent to Frontex’s activity. This set of warnings in fact led 

Agency to suspend the development of PeDRA project, its initiative for the exchange of personal data 

with Europol for risk analysis29. 

Risk management (first and foremost on irregular migration) undoubtedly determines the regulatory 

design and functioning of Frontex30, constituting its very strategic focus31. The Agency’s risk assessment 

seeks to profile patterns and trends in irregular migratory flows and (on what should be a secondary level) 

cross-border criminal activities, with the aim of planning resource allocation, operations and border 

controls themselves32. Frontex has developed a «Common Integrated Risk Analysis Model» (CIRAM) for 

this purpose33. Currently in its version 2.1, it seeks to «establish a clear and transparent methodology for 

risk analysis to facilitate effective information exchange and cooperation in the field of border security», 

and it is meant to provide three types of risk analysis: general annual, specific for planning «operational 

activities» and strategic for multiannual policy planning [article 29(2) of Frontex Regulation]34. 

For Frontex, risk is defined as «the magnitude and likelihood of a threat that may affect the management 

of the external borders»35. The functionality of this type of risk analysis is primarily planning around 

«intelligence». At this level, its methodology has been mainly qualitative with occasional recourse to 

quantification, or a combinations of both36. In different ways, space is thought of as a surface furrowed 

by traces that can be imagined or calculated in their virtuality. But the results of this type of analysis

become more concrete in the exercise of controls at external border crossings in the tasks of «border 

inspection»37. This is where the police problem of specific surveillance and selection in controls and the 

managerial dialectic of fluidity-security emerges. «It involves assessing which people, vehicles, boats or 

flights to check», an assessment that «is made in a real tactical situation, which is why the methods can 

be called tactical risk analysis»38. In this projection, the technological deployment of the border aims to 

compensate for the pace of the bodies in circulation with the speed of data transmission and its just-in-

time application, segmented and profiled according to assessments on the ground39. 

Frontex’s analytical output therefore operates on two levels. On one level, it seeks to anticipate trends in 

migratory routes and identify risks on a, shall we say, «systemic» scale. On another level, it is aimed at 

informing the concrete control and decision-making work of border officials on border crossings. In the 

first case, risk is approached as an essentially qualitative entity and constructed from diverse epistemic 

perspectives. In the second case, we can observe, the quantitative and standardised inclination of risk 

assessment revealed by the ETIAS. 

As we will see infra, the Agency assumes a main role in the ETIAS screening device. In fact, the regulation 

of this system represents the first foresight of an EU’s IT system whose central unit will be hosted and 

managed by Frontex40. Even more so, the transformation of the VIS in parallel to the ETIAS has also 

subsequently entailed the assumption of competences by the ETIAS Central Unit over the risk 

assessment mechanism for visa purposes41. 

 

3. The architecture of the ETIAS 

The regime for crossing external borders is based on two types of selective criteria. Firstly, it applies static 

criteria relating to nationality, discriminating between the general application of a «minimum check» for 

holders of free movement within the EU and the «thorough» check foreseen for TCNs42. Secondly, 

dynamic assessments on the flow of movement are applied, aimed at planning more intensive controls: 

either «second line» inspections of TCNs or checks on the dangerousness of holders of freedom of

The ETIAS must be understood at the intersection of these governance needs (static and dynamic 

segmentation of mobility and structuring of police assessments) and a preventive (risk-oriented) and 

technological inclination (aiming at intelligent filtering of border traffic). The ETIAS is also an expression 

of the trend towards the extra-territorialisation of the border, that is, the attempt to control the 

movements of travellers prior to arrival at border crossing points by transforming the traditional 

approach to border inspection45, thus seeking to implement what has been termed «remote control»46. It 

is an efficiency-based logic that nevertheless has profound legal impacts as it conditions ex ante the 

possibility of movement and the exercise of rights in the face of the border decision (the consequences 

can be particularly serious, for example, in the case of asylum seekers). The mechanism articulated by the 

ETIAS Regulation will make access to the Schengen area for TCNs exempt from the visa requirement 

subject to prior authorisation. The new system creates for them an authorisation regime parallel to the 

visa regime, based on cross-checks in databases and a risk assessment that modulates the administrative 

procedure in algorithmic terms. 

The model is based on the previous experiences of Australian ETA, United States ESTA and Canadian 

eTA systems47, cited in the Proposal for the Regulation of the ETIAS to support the «rationale for the 

setting up» of the new system48. Although the idea of incorporating an «European ESTA» was first raised

assessment and management mechanism, which enables a «dynamic» governance of border transit. 

 

3.1. Organisational structure and administrative-technological procedure 

The relevant Regulation creates a complex organisational structure whose central bodies are attached to 

Frontex and a whole technical infrastructure for which the Agency for the Operational Management of 

Large-Scale IT Systems in the AFSJ (eu-LISA) is responsible50. Articles 5 to 10 of the Regulation 

distribute the functions of risk assessment in this organisational complex: an «information system» 

consisting of a «central system», an interface and various telematic access services and applications; a 

«central unit», integrated in Frontex, with responsibilities for updating the system’s data, the «specific risk 

indicators» on which the assessment is based, and with regular auditing functions51; the «national units» 

designated in each Member State, which are responsible for examining applications for travel 

authorisations in cases of «manual processing» and deciding on their granting, refusal, cancellation or 

revocation52; and two advisory/assessment bodies53.

Based on this organisational structure, the system works as follows54.  

Before travelling, the applicant must fill in an online application with a set of personal data according to 

a standardised form55. They must also answer a series of questions about their criminal record, previous 

travel to certain parts of the world and if they have previously been expelled from an EU member country. 

If the answer to any of these is positive, additional questions not specified in the Regulation must be 

answered. 

The application is subject to automated processing which involves submitting it to a battery of checks 

provided for in Article 20 of the Regulation, including background information, previous travel to conflict 

zones and previous expulsion orders issued by a Member State. The data in the request is checked against 

various databases (SIS, VIS, SES, Eurodac, Europol and Interpol: SLTD and TDAWN)56 via the 

European Search Portal (ESP)57. In addition, the applicant’s details are checked against the «ETIAS 

watchlist», a database in the central ETIAS system containing information on persons «who are suspected 

of having committed or taken part in a terrorist offence or other serious criminal offence» or in respect 

of whom «there are factual indications or reasonable grounds, based on an overall assessment of the 

person, to believe that they will commit» such offences (Article 34, emphasis added). As can be seen, inclusion 

on the watchlist is a kind of risk assessment nested in the ETIAS risk management system. Finally, the 

information concerning the applicant is compared with the specific risk indicators to determine the risk 

level of the applicant, according to the ETIAS screening rules (regulated in Article 33) which aim to 

quantitatively measure the threat level of the applicant in three ways: the security risk, the illegal 

immigration risk and the high epidemic risk attributable to the subject under consideration. This is the 

automated and quantitative risk calculation that is the algorithmic core of the system. 

If the above checks do not produce any positive response (i.e., no database match, no affirmative 

response, no match with the watchlist and no assessment of high-risk level), the travel authorisation is 

issued automatically. Conversely, hits for any of the above reasons require additional checks according 

to the steps below.

A positive response means that the case is subject to verification by the ETIAS Central Unit, which will 

check the match detected in the automated processing (Article 22). If a false positive is found, the travel 

authorisation will be issued automatically; if the positive is confirmed or doubts remain as to the identity 

of the applicant, «manual processing» will be carried out by the relevant ETIAS national unit58. This 

mechanism seeks to ensure that no refusal can be based on automated processing alone (Recital 25). 

In case of a referral for manual processing, the competent National Unit will have to decide whether to 

issue or refuse the travel authorisation according to criteria which vary depending on the reason for the 

positive outcome (Article 26).  

In some cases, the National Unit is entrusted with an additional assessment of security risk, illegal 

immigration and/or high epidemic risk. The methodology of this new risk assessment is not specified in 

the regulation. Furthermore, the National Unit may request additional information or documentation 

from the applicant in order to make its assessment (Article 27). It is also allowed «in exceptional 

circumstances and as a last resort» to summon the applicant for an interview in his/her country of 

residence (through the Consulate) or virtually. If, even after an interview, doubts remain as to the level 

of threat posed by the subject or at the request of another Member State, the National Unit may issue 

the authorisation «with a recommendation to the border authorities to carry out a second line check» 

[Article 36(2)]. 

It should be noted that the issuing of a travel authorisation does not guarantee entry into the Schengen 

area. Even with verified valid authorisation, and even without any indication of a second line check, 

border officials «must decide whether to authorise or refuse entry» for which they will have access to the 

data in the file on the subject (Recital 37). In other words, the standardisation of risk assessment 

implemented by the Regulation does not exclude the possibility of qualitative or speculative police 

judgement. 

Moreover, there is the possibility of subsequent annulment and revocation of the authorisation granted 

by the competent National Unit (Articles 40 and 41). Annulment occurs when it is established that the 

authorisation granted was, at the time it was issued, in breach of one of the grounds for refusal provided 

for in the Regulation. Revocation occurs when «the conditions for issuing it are no longer met», that is, 

when the grounds for refusal are supervened.  This may be due to the introduction of new alerts in the 

SIS, SES or the ETIAS watchlist generating overlaps with an issued authorisation, in which case a new 

security risk assessment by the National Unit would be required. The provision does not refer to a 

possible revocation due to overlap with specific risk indicators, that is, due to a change in the level of risk

The possibility of permanent review of the authorization implies an extension of administrative police 

powers that responds to what Brouwer has called «the multiplication of borders» (here towards the 

interior of the European territory)59. This trend is also reflected in the increase in police checks linked to 

border management within the territory of the Member States, in a logic that blurs the distinction between 

external border checks and (non-systematic) checks for other police purposes. It is a differentiation clearly 

stipulated in Article 23 of the Schengen Borders Code but questioned by that tendency to expand 

controls60, as the new Screening Regulation seems to encourage.  

Finally, if a traveller’s application is rejected, they are granted the right to appeal against decisions of 

refusal, annulment and revocation [Articles 37(3), 40(3) and 41(7) of the ETIAS Regulation, respectively]. 

Appeals must in any case be lodged with the National Units which adopted them.

Border control has traditionally operated using «blacklists» (undesirable travellers subject to banning), 

«green lists» (trusted travellers subject to minimal checks) and «grey lists» where control is modulated 

more flexibly according to the degree of suspicion of travellers61. The latter is the area of border police 

discretion par excellence. Devices such as the ETIAS seem to partially alter this classical structure. While 

maintaining the logic of blacklisting through cross-checking with databases, they blur the distinction 

between green and grey zones by subjecting all access applicants to risk assessment. Rather, it is this

assessment, as a quantitative estimate of suspicion, that dynamically modulates the distinction between 

those situations on a scale that can range from unacceptable risk (which implies a new blacklisting as a 

result) to no significant risk (comparable to green listing, without further cross-checks) or intermediate 

risk zones (which may imply additional information, new assessments or more intensive checks). But the 

cut-off points on this scale are not known a priori, at least by the subjects concerned, and they are not 

static, but variable depending on the risk assessment criteria and their computation. Moreover, a person’s 

status on the scale is not definitive either, but subject to change as risk is reassessed. 

On the other hand, the ETIAS combines two temporal logics, controls based on past behaviour and 

future-oriented forecasting analysis62. We cannot consider this combination per se as a novelty in border 

controls, but rather the way in which it is articulated and the way in which the prognostic judgement is 

structured. In the following section, I look at this double logic of control and its legal configuration in 

more detail. 

 

3.2. The profiling engine: screening rules and risk indicators 

The ETIAS formally implements an automated profiling-type control of border transit. In fact, it is the 

first regulation in European law of this kind of management devices. In terms of the specific assessment 

it proposes, the ETIAS claims to be able to detect the threat posed by a person for three types of risk 

which are listed in detail63: 

⸺ Security risk: the risk of a threat to public policy, internal security or the international relations of 

any of the Member States.  

⸺ Illegal immigration risk: the threat that a TCN does not fulfil the conditions of entry and stay.  

⸺ And high epidemic risk: the risk posed by a person in view of any disease with epidemic potential, 

infectious or contagious parasitic diseases. 

The Regulation provides for the procedure for defining and specifying these general risks by means of 

«risk indicators». This specification process will be carried out through delegated and executive acts of 

the Commission and the ETIAS Central Unit itself integrated in Frontex. Finally, an algorithm (not 

specified in the Regulation) will provide the attributed risk profile by cross-checking the traveller’s 

personal information against the pre-set specific risk indicators64. 

The standard outlines the process through which risk assessment takes place, but also how the parameters 

governing risk assessment are to be specified with detailed organisational attributions. The legal elements 

of the process of building an algorithmic model are regulated. The outline is as follows:

In short, the Commission, by means of a delegated act, determines «with greater precision» the risks 

regarding security, illegal immigration or epidemics on the basis of the data and statistical information 

specified in Article 33(2) of the Regulation. On this basis, by an implementing act, the Commission 

identifies the specific risks to be considered on which the specific risk indicators will be based. Finally, 

the ETIAS Central Unit will have to establish the list of specific risk indicators consisting of a certain 

combination of data. 

As can be seen, the system amalgamates a wide range of threats and affirms the technological ability to 

pinpoint the likelihood of generic and distinct potential behaviours and circumstances. Security risks are 

hardly defined in the regulation. They seem to cover, at least potentially, any type of offence, whether 

administrative or criminal, but also behaviour that is not defined in punitive law, but which may be 

considered a threat to public policy, internal security or international relations. Even regarding criminal 

offences, the Regulation contains few predeterminations. For the purposes of the information required 

in the application form, the criminal records to be communicated are restricted to the twenty-nine 

offences included in the Annex and to a period of the previous ten years or twenty in the case of terrorist 

offences [Article 17(4)(a)]. Even so, it should be noted that there are no territorial limits on the previous

It should be clarified that the specification of risks is a periodic task and indicators are always provisional. 

They will be reviewed at least every six months by means of new implementing acts. In addition, the list 

of specific risk indicators must be assessed ex ante and evaluated ex post and periodically revised (and 

deleted) by the Central Unit, after consulting the ETIAS or VIS Screening Boards70. 

As can be seen, both the ETIAS regulation and, later, the new configuration of the VIS, provide for the 

construction of a profiling engine but do so in formal rather than substantive terms. The fundamental 

aspects of the specification of the model are not found in the Regulations but are referred to in 

subsequent delegated and implementing decisions and even in decisional acts of a dubious normative 

nature. This scheme raises doubts from the point of view of the rule of law and the system of sources of 

European law, as will be discussed in the next section.  

 

4. A blank technological regulation 

Over and above the legal architecture of the mechanism and its procedural particularities, the ETIAS 

merits more comprehensive reflections on the «regulatory style» it establishes. Regulations on large-scale 

IT systems in the AFSJ, together with provisions for their interoperability, have led to a legal over-

complexity71. The possibility to process information is being overlaid by standards with a convoluted 

architecture. Within this complexity, the formality of the system of sources of law is shaken by the 

technological delegation that affects its very core. 

The ETIAS Regulation refers central aspects of its operation to an external digital model. It should be 

noted that it is not an auxiliary software that facilitates the execution of the analogical regulation, but a 

real «normative technology» with legal decision-making effects72. It is a technical object expressly designed 

for the purpose of regulating behaviour, which also determines the meaning of a legally enforceable act. 

However, the Regulation is broadly undetermined in technological terms. Risk indicators and the 

algorithm that will process them are postponed to a later date when they will be decided. This means that 

it is not yet possible to determine the kind of data modelling it will be based on, that is, whether the 

system will be based on a «classical» actuarial risk model73 or on some kind of AI technology (some form

The CJEU has already expressed the view that profiling by public authorities must be carried out 

according to pre-determined criteria78. The question that now arises is what legal requirements should be 

applied to this task of prior determination. Such a discussion becomes particularly relevant if we consider 

the ETIAS Regulation as a kind of regulatory laboratory for the formal implementation of algorithmic 

technologies in the AFSJ. Obviously, the analysis in this respect applies equally to the parallel VIS risk 

assessment regime. 

 

4.1. Substantive legality issues 

From the perspective of the principle of legality, understood in the first sense as substantive legality, the 

limits of delegation are determined by Articles 290 and 291 TFEU, according to which the «essential 

elements» of the regulation must be contained in the legislative act and the terms of the delegation must 

be made explicit by it79. The delegated act cannot make the political choices inherent in the responsibilities 

of the EU legislator, so it cannot affect the essential rules of the regulation. The CJEU has understood 

such attribution in the sense that this political character depends, essentially, on the existence of various 

options linked to different interests that require weighting80. This was the Court’s approach, for example, 

in the case of the regulation of surveillance activities at external borders in the Schengen Borders Code 

of 200681 and its development by the Council through an implementing decision82. For the Court, the 

general legal authorisation to adopt «additional measures relating to surveillance» did not cover the 

delegated regulation that specified in detail a list of measures to be applied by border guards on 

intercepted persons and vessels in maritime surveillance work83. Using this technique, essential elements 

of the regulation of the matter were transferred outside the Regulation itself. The reason is that this 

specification of measures required «the conflicting interests at issue to be weighed up on the basis of a

In the case of the ETIAS Regulation, the standard includes a definition of risks with a very broad scope. 

The significance of these risks is only defined through subsequent decisions conferred on the

It cannot be ignored, however, that the centrality of risk in the case of the ETIAS Regulation could 

qualify the problem. Here, the friction with the principle of legality ultimately expresses the problem of 

«material determinations» that usually affects regulatory law in terms of the specification of risks. As 

Esteve Pardo has pointed out, the laws delimiting the permitted risk in different sectors of regulation do 

not usually contain such determinations; instead, those laws are limited to defining the procedural and 

competence aspects for their specification, which, in the case of the substantive ones, is entrusted to the 

administrative authority86. However, this risk-induced regulatory logic is characteristic of technological 

regulation as an object or target of law. That is, the broad delegation of material determinations outside 

the law refers to the assessment of the risks that a technological development carries and the decision 

about its degree of acceptability. It is more problematic to project this regulatory approach onto cases in 

which technology is regulated, not as a risky object external to the norm, but as the mechanism that the 

law itself adopts to assess human behaviour. We are talking here about public powers structured by means 

of a technological device for human evaluation. Nor should this weakening of the rule of law be 

understood as inevitable under a generic appeal to preventive or precautionary security needs87. 

It can be hypothesised that Frontex’s specialisation as a risk analysis hub generates a certain attractive 

force that affects the legal technique. This seems to be corroborated by the evolution of the case of

4.2. Frontex empowerment and its limits 

With these assumptions, the second aspect of delegation leads to the Frontex functions on the profiling 

engine. More broadly, it confronts us with the topic of «operational tasks» as a functional specificity of 

Agencies, a slogan that, as Tassinari says, has been more repeated than scrutinised in its meaning94. The 

delimitation of these attributions becomes more pressing in the context of agencification of EU 

administration and increasing direct administrative functions, as the case of Frontex reveals95. The 

difficulty in establishing the limits of the tasks that can be entrusted to an Agency relates mainly to those 

involving decisions with a certain degree of discretion. Here the notion of the «operational» becomes 

somewhat blurred. 

This question has been guided by European case-law since the well-known Meroni case and the evolution 

of its doctrine96. The latest stage, the ESMA-short selling case97, seems to have relaxed the classical 

restrictions on delegation to Agencies through an expansive interpretation of the notion of «technical 

discretion», accepting wide margins of non-political discretion in favour of Agencies (being such if there 

is a precise delimitation of the decision to be adopted and it is accessible for judicial review)98. However, 

in our opinion, the evolution of the case law does not remedy the problems that the ETIAS Regulation 

exhibits from the point of view of the limits of the Agencies’ discretion. 

The problem must be analysed along two dimensions that can be deduced from the aforementioned 

jurisprudence. On the one hand, the discretionary decision can be considered in the dimension of its 

variables (number, diversity and scope of the interests at stake, which determines the complexity of the 

weighting that underpins the decision). This is the criterion that, following Meroni, draws the line between 

political decision-making powers (non-delegable) and mere implementation powers (attributable to 

Agencies), which are always predetermined by the former. The ESMA-short selling judgment does not 

question this diagnosis but seems to link the label of «technical implementation» to a sufficient degree of 

delimitation of the discretionary decision by the enabling rule: the technical decision must take place in 

strictly delimited circumstances which restrict the complexity of the balancing at stake. 

The case of the ETIAS Regulation hardly passes this first filter. As we have argued, the initial delegation 

to the Commission raises doubts about the transfer of decisions that are materially legislative. We are

a problem of successive instances of vagueness that makes it impossible to know, from the point of view

On the other hand, the discretionary decision can be considered in its legal nature depending on whether 

it has normative effects. It is relevant here to refer to the doctrine of the Romano case from which it 

follows that European Agencies cannot be empowered to adopt normative acts99. The ESMA-short 

selling judgement has not corrected this doctrine. In it, the Court merely confirmed that it is permissible 

for bodies and Agencies of the Union to adopt acts of general application. Note, however, that this is 

not synonymous with normative acts. Following an objective criterion and not the overly simple 

argument of the subjective scope of the act, the normative character generally depends on the abstraction 

of the cases covered by the act.  The decisive fact is that it is repeatable over time as opposed to the 

singularity of the non-normative acts which limits its application to a discrete number of cases. This is so 

although some formal types of acts, such as Decisions in European law, can be ambivalent in this 

regard100. Discretion can be projected with varying intensity in this range from individualised acts of 

different kinds to the formulation of general standards101. 

In the ESMA case, the discretionary decision was admissible because it involved the assessment of certain 

types of cases in the light of the criteria predefined by a Regulation. This sufficient predetermination of 

the conditions of the act made it a technical decision. We can now see how the application of legal criteria 

to specific cases (albeit with an a priori indeterminate scope in terms of the number of subjects 

concerned) excluded its normative nature. The case of the ETIAS Regulation is, however, very different. 

The attribution of discretion to Frontex no longer consists of the capacity to assess certain legal criteria 

that may affect a generality of cases of a certain type. The delegation now consists of the Agency’s capacity 

to dictate the final criteria that will determine administrative decisions in the objective and subjective 

sphere of the Regulation. In other words, Frontex is entrusted with the specification of a blank regulation 

in a large part of its essential content. It is therefore entrusted with fully normative tasks.

This empowerment contrasts, for example, with the explicitly non-regulatory character attributed to the

preserve the normative functions reserved to the Commission in the regulation of such systems102. This 

precaution is not evident in the case of the ETIAS Regulation, which rather blurs the limits of the 

principle of legality considering the powers of the Frontex Agency.  

In short, the ETIAS (and the new VIS) Regulation seems to assume algorithmic development and the 

prior specification of risks as a purely technical task that can be taken out of the sphere of action of the 

European legislator and even executed by an Agency outside the orthodox sources of law. Musco Eklund 

has described this drift as a new form of algorithmic discretion that slips into arbitrariness103. Ultimately, 

the problem lies with the insufficient consideration of the normative meaning that the algorithm acquires 

in this type of decision-making context. It is relevant to consider that an algorithm that fulfils this 

functionality is part of the regulation itself; that technology itself operates as a source of law104. 

 

5. The exercise of public powers by algorithmic means at the European level 

The lack of consideration of the legal meaning of algorithmic technology is not compensated through 

the general framework of European law for automated decisions. This regulatory framework does not 

seem to assume the specificity of such technologies when used by public authorities, nor does it consider 

the mutations that these technologies induce into the system of sources of law. Until recently, the only 

hard law provisions on profiling and the use of automated technologies at the European level were to be 

found in sectoral regulations on personal data protection. Now, the so called «AI Act» provides a more 

comprehensive legal framework for the uses of digital systems incorporating AI105. However, its approach 

still has shortcomings from the perspective of software that operates as normative technologies in public 

law. Moreover, its applicability to the ETIAS is not self-evident. 

 

5.1. ETIAS as automated data processing

This is a clear example of how technical solutionism may produce an anamorphosis effect on legal 

categories (it distorts their meaning by focusing on them in an altered way). The creation of a centralised, 

all-encompassing database on TCNs (a possibly unfeasible solution in legal and political terms) would 

eliminate the principle of purpose limitation. The alternative solution of generating functional bridges 

between large-scale databases makes difficult trade-offs on that principle through a system of hists and 

successive authorisations. It creates a complex and problematic regime from the point of view of the 

clarity and taxativity of purposes required by Articles 6 GDPR, 8 LED and 5 of Regulation 2018/1725125. 

Without being excluded, the principle of limitation is broken down and recomposed under the imperative 

of new processing functionalities. 

 

5.2. A high-risk AI system? 

The recently adopted AI Act provides a new regulatory framework that may subsume some forms of 

profiling under public law. The objective scope of the rule is based on a proper definition of what is to 

be considered as artificial intelligence for these purposes. According to Article 3(1) (in conjunction with 

Article 2) it regulates algorithmic technologies which are: a machine-based system designed to operate with a 

certain level of autonomy, which exhibit adaptiveness after deployment, and which infers, from the input 

information it receives, how to generate outputs in the form of predictions influencing, in this case, 

physical environments. 

It is impossible to say if the ETIAS risk assessment algorithm will meet the requirements of this 

definition. Its nature as a blank technological regulation means that its legal regime is indeterminate until 

it is developed. Although the characteristics of the system might suggest an AI-based solution, there is 

also some room for doubt that this will be so126. 

CJEU case-law on the treatment of PNR could support misgivings about the feasibility of an AI 

development for the ETIAS. In its 2022 judgment «Ligue de droits humains», the Court excludes the use 

of machine learning systems for the screening of air passenger data for the purposes of the «PNR 

Directive»127. The reason is the impact that such technologies, given their inherent opacity, would have 

on the legal guarantees of the persons concerned. Both in terms of the effective possibility of review of

Some analyses have posited the extrapolation of this doctrine to the ETIAS case, inferring a 

jurisprudential refusal to apply AI systems with machine learning capabilities129. These legal problems of 

ML technologies are explicit in the judgement and certainly evident in a hypothetical implementation of 

the ETIAS under these parameters. However, the reasoning of the Court is based on a rule applicable to 

the «Ligue de droits humains» case that is not present in the ETIAS Regulation. Article 6(3) of the PNR 

Directive expressly requires that admissible criminal risk assessments on such data be carried out either 

(a) by comparisons with databases relevant for such purposes or (b) other processing «against pre-

determined criteria». It is this last requirement that led the Court to reject algorithmic models that 

generate assessment criteria autonomously from their training work. It is no longer a question of the 

processing having purposes pre-established by regulation [in the terms of Article 8(2) of the Charter of 

Fundamental Rights of the EU (CFREU) and Article 6(2) of the PNR Directive itself], but of the 

processing being carried out in accordance with predetermined criteria within the permitted purposes. 

Despite its technological indeterminacy, there is no requirement in the ETIAS Regulation for a precise 

determination of the treatment criteria beyond the general guidelines for setting the risk indicators and 

drawing up the algorithm. This omission, apart from the problems of legality it raises for the Regulation, 

could make it difficult to extrapolate the reasoning of the Court in the «Ligue de droits humains» case. 

On the other hand, unlike the PNR case, which is aimed at prevention and criminal prosecution, the 

ETIAS articulates an administrative authorisation outside the walls of the punitive order. If we consider 

the accesses allowed to the ETIAS for law enforcement purposes by Chapter X of its Regulation, this 

refers to data stored in the system for processing but not apparently to the results of the subsequent 

assessments. This difference in purpose and normative fields may pose an additional obstacle to the wider 

projection of the PNR doctrine.  

The application of the AI Act to the ETIAS can only be analysed hypothetically for these objective 

reasons but also because of the transitional regime of the new regulation. It should be noted that Article 

111(1) of the Act provides for a long adaptation period for «large-scale» IT systems listed in Annex X 

(including the ETIAS).  For systems to be implemented before 2 August 2027 (as is likely to be the case 

for ETIAS), the compliance with the requirements of the new standard will have to take place by the end 

of 2030. In a field affected by a growing technological acceleration, such a deadline (almost 52 months

Despite these warnings, if we assume the hypothesis of the likely incorporation of AI technologies into 

the ETIAS and, thus, the (very belated) subjection of the system to the AI Act, there is one main issue 

that needs to be addressed:  the possible friction of certain aspects of the ETIAS Regulation with some 

of the practices prohibited by Article 5 of the AI Act. 

On the one hand, paragraph (1)(c) prohibits the use of AI systems to assess individuals on the basis of 

their social behaviour or inferred or predicted personal characteristics, if the result causes either 

detrimental or unfavourable treatment: (i) in social contexts that are unrelated to the contexts where the 

data were originally generated or collected; or (ii) in a way that is unjustified or disproportionate to their 

social behaviour or its gravity. The breadth and diversity of data on which the ETIAS profiling engine 

will draw raises concerns about the production of the first of the prohibited effects. Interoperability risks 

incurring such detrimental effects arising from the translation of data between different contexts and 

purposes. And the definition of the model’s indicators will have to respect the requirements of relevance 

and proportionality required to avoid the second of the effects considered. 

On the other hand, paragraph (1)(d) of Article 5 prohibits the use of AI for risk assessment of natural 

persons to predict the risk of committing criminal offences if such assessments are based solely on the 

profiling of that person or the assessment of that person’s personality traits and characteristics. The 

acceptability of the ETIAS’ security profiling (including crime risk assessment) could perhaps be justified 

by the fact that crime prediction is not the purpose of the system but an ingredient of the more complex 

assessment that operates for other purposes. However, note that the wording of this the prohibition does 

not require that the risk assessment is carried out for criminal law enforcement purposes but only that 

the system is used to predict the likelihood of criminal offences. Despite all this, the diversity of data that 

will feed the risk indicators may also circumvent the prohibition, as they are not exclusively based on 

personal characteristics of individuals but also on objective circumstances affecting them (such as 

criminal records or previous conduct). 

It is not my intention to assert the incompatibility of the ETIAS with the prohibitions proposed by the 

AI Act. I intend to point out the need for their compatibility to be justified, at least in the aspects 

mentioned.  

In any case, the ETIAS would be considered a «high-risk AI system»130. The regulation reiterates this 

status for systems used for a wide variety of purposes in the areas of migration, asylum and border control 

management. This qualification will subject the system to the ex-ante evaluation processes foreseen in the

certain forms of rationality that cut across and shape it. It responds to a way of objectifying the 

phenomenon of migration (or international mobility); a way of focusing the work of border legal

In the first direction, the ETIAS is incorporated into the increasingly preventive, technology-driven and 

managerial fabric of the EU’s external borders, whose resources weave a veritable web of devices. The 

ETIAS not only draws on data provided by other systems and gadgets but, as we know, also contributes 

to their operability. Access to the system is mainly foreseen for border verification purposes (Article 47 

of the Regulation) but it is also allowed for national immigration authorities in their own area of 

management (Article 49) and for law enforcement purposes by national authorities and Europol (Articles 

12 and 50 to 53). As we have argued, this approach raises questions about the principles of purpose 

limitation and minimization in the processing of personal data. As noted by EDPS, this approach is also 

an expression of the «increasing trend in EU policymaking to associate migration management and 

security purposes»139. 

However, that connection seems to be the strategic priority, at least as defined by Frontex and Europol 

(in the so-called Future Group), whose aim is «to pro-actively bring the different professional disciplines 

(…) to see how to maximize de operational benefits», facilitating the use of border and migration 

management information in support of criminal investigations and vice versa140. An aim always 

streamlined by technological means141. Under the idea of «travel continuum», the legal concept of 

competence is modulated: each authority (whether National or European) involved in the management 

of international mobility (no longer border control, migration, asylum or law enforcement) should be 

aware that it is part of an overarching set of measures. On this continuum, moreover, decisions are always 

provisional in the light of the data: «the identification, security screening and risk management are iterative 

processes applied at different steps when new information about the individual is made available»142. 

This inertia towards interoperability and technological efficiency responds in large part to what Bellanova 

and Gouftsios have called «database anxieties», i.e. «epistemic and operational concerns emerging from 

[European institutions’] appreciation of the current use of databases»143; in particular, concerns about the 

risk of under-utilisation of all the capabilities offered by the data already at hand or that may become 

available.

In the second direction noted, the ETIAS model is based on the idea that it is possible to reduce the 

scope for discretion in border policing by means of technical routines. Algorithm application displaces 

intuitive or «hand-crafted» policing knowledge in favour of automated, quantitative analysis. It 

standardises decision-making, and, in this way, it brings an objective motivation to a decision affected by 

a high degree of discretion. The ETIAS screening attempts to synchronise the border decision with the 

pace imposed by certain technical objects. It does not change the degree of discretion of the border 

decision, but it alters the very meaning of the term. It moves away from the broad «police know-how» 

and attaches itself to the formal objectivity of technological application144. 

Lastly, in the third direction pointed out, the normative technology of the ETIAS articulates anticipatory 

judgments about behaviour. It induces preventive decisions that process subjects as sources of probable 

threat. It is a case of behavioural algorithm that takes on a pseudo-punitive character, at least in a material 

sense. Although the ETIAS regulation aims to separate its administrative use from its criminal law 

utilities, it is clear that the distinction is blurred in the complex operation of the system. This is particularly 

the case at problematic points such as the ETIAS watching list145. 

In fact, behavioural forecasts have long been present in border and migration management under a lax 

notion of dangerousness, which has intertwined threats to public order and safety, national security and 

public health. But the ETIAS algorithmic model redefines the meaning of this prognosis, transforming 

the traditionally qualitative and low systematic estimates into a quantitative forecasting model, highly 

structured and operated by automated or semi-automated means. Its probabilistic basis assumes a 

rationality of epidemiological tone: it seeks patterns at the population level in order to individualize the 

legal response according to group factors and ascriptions. The way in which patterns are identified and 

ascriptions are made will depend on the type of modelling that is implemented. In any case, this kind of 

justice quantifies the prognosis and eschews causal reasoning. It does not operate on the basis of causal 

relationships but on the basis of correlative ones. 

In this way, the ETIAS assumes an individualized risk judgment but not an individual one. It does not 

start from the subject as an individual but as a dividual being broken down into factors146. In short, 

algorithmic learning of the «mathematical truth» implicit in the data, perhaps unobservable by analogical 

means, would allow prognostic judgments to be projected onto new situations in the same field. 

                                                           
144 This redefinition has been noted by, among others, T. DEKKERS, M. VAN DER WOUDE and R. KOULISH, 
Objectivity and accountability in migration control using risk assessment tools, in European Journal of Criminology, vol. 16(2), 2018, pp. 
3-4.; and D. BIGO, Interoperability…, cit., pp. 400-401.  
145 This problem has been highlighted by F. MICHEA and L. ROUSVOAL, The Criminal..., cit.  
146 N. GÄCKLE, Taming…, cit., p. 261.

In general, profiling is a technique that aims to manage the «overabundance» of information in order to 

extract meaning from it and guide decisions147. Operationally, it involves creating «virtual people» based 

on data analysis, which are then re-incarnated on the profiled individuals148. These virtualization-

concretion dynamics are accelerated by the advance of machine learning and big data processing, capable 

of generating constantly reformulated momentary groupings149. In reality, this type of statistical model 

does not immediately deal with people but with information about them with which to operate 

inferences150. Therefore, the assessment through abstract risk factors dissolves the notion of subject. It 

does not identify a precise danger embedded in an individual; it deconstructs and recomposes it as a 

factorial combination151, as a dividual case152. Algorithmic profiling defines a form of nomothetic justice 

rather than an idiographic justice, with the consequent problem of formalisation of discrimination and 

bias. 

The approach is based on an open pragmatism. Faced with the drawbacks of case-by-case behavioural 

predictions, the model is satisfied with estimating the probability that «individuals with a given 

configuration of risk markers» will manifest the behaviour under consideration153. It is accompanied, 

moreover, by the promise of reliability that quantitative methodologies offer and, and even more so when 

it happens, by a certain mysticism that surrounds AI developments. Rouvroy early on described this 

epistemic inclination as «data behaviourism», understood as a radicalisation of inductive reasoning based 

on the wide availability of data that tends to confuse a certain form of knowability with «reality itself»154. 

However, the ETIAS approach is the expression of a reliance on processing capabilities that pushes the 

very concept of prediction to its limits155. The regulation contemplates a huge disparity of behaviours
