under the different dimensions of risk, some of which are dependent on normative definitions with wide 

variations even within the European context. Moreover, many of these behaviours lack a theoretical 

framework of meaningful predictors (unlike, for example, specific forms of criminal behaviour such as 

gender-based and sexual violence). The delimitation of such epistemic ambition will depend on the 

specification of the model in successive acts of delegation, implementation and definition of risk 

indicators. But the limits of machinic prediction should not be ignored; as Buchanan has warned, those 

limits always depend on the trade-offs between modelling and strict quantitative analysis and on the 

dimensionality of the system under consideration156. 

 

6.2. Effective remedies to algorithmic decision-making 

In addition to these concerns, the hypothetical operational advantages of the system must be weighed 

against the legal drawbacks that are also inherent to these forms of legal automation in many ways. It 

cannot be ignored that, in the case of border management, algorithmic rationality impacts on a peculiar 

space of administrative legality due to its sharp preventive and instrumental focus. Thus, pre-entry 

procedures themselves pose legal problems from the point of view of due process because of the 

relocation of administrative control that they entail157. The ETIAS regulation incorporates 

counterweights to these potential problems arising from the confluence of algorithmic action and border 

and migration management. However, there are doubts about the effectiveness of these remedies. 

We have already noted that the diversity of data considered and the laxity in the normative 

predetermination of risk factors casts doubt, at least a priori, on the real possibility of controlling for 

disparate impact effects by proxy influences among the variables in the model. The EDPS expressly 

warned of the effect that data on nationality, country and city of residence [Article 17(2)(f) of the ETIAS 

Regulation] may have on the protected categories of ethnic or racial origin, especially when those data 

are combined with other data; or the effect that data on current occupation [Article 17(2)(i)] may have 

on trade union membership158. Moreover, the intended use of aggregate data on «abnormal rates of 

refusals» for certain population groups [Article 33.2.(a)] risks reproducing and multiplying historical 

biases that may exist for such groups159.

In addition, the possibilities for subjects to react legally to the ETIAS profiling engine may be limited. 

The problems in substantiating objections to ETIAS profiling are due to the architecture of the system, 

the rationality that underpins the system’s decision, and the way in which it could be motivated. 

As can be seen in the organisational chart of the system, there is a separation in the ETIAS between the 

definition of the processing model and the final decision making, at least in cases of refusal. As we know, 

the risk indicators that will determine the profile will be based on data provided by the different Member 

States and other bodies, in accordance with modelling carried out by the Central Unit within Frontex. 

However, manual processing in cases of negative automated decisions and the final decision following 

such a review is the responsibility of the National Units of the system, which are part of the 

Administration of each Member State (Article 22 of the ETIAS Regulation).  Accordingly, pursuant to 

Articles 37(3), 40(3) and 41(7) of the Regulation, appeals against decisions in authorisation, annulment 

and revocation procedures must be conducted in the Member State that adopted them through its ETIAS 

National Unit and in accordance with its own national law. It is questionable whether the national 

authorities can review a decision based on technical parameters that they have not established and which 

they may only partially now160. A doubt that will obviously increase if a machine learning model is finally 

implemented161. Moreover, as is well known, the likelihood for a human operator rejecting the outcome 

of an «objective» algorithmic decision is rather small162, especially when the algorithmic system yields a 

higher risk than perceived by the human subject163. It should be noted that the human reviewer is only 

«looped in» in extremis, when the algorithmic decision has already been handed down164 and without the 

subject concerned being able to intervene in any way beforehand in the procedure. In terms of control 

of the algorithm it is a strict case of «human after the loop».  

All of which leads to limitations in the effectiveness of human oversight on the performance of the 

algorithmic system, which in turn erodes the requirements of Article 22(3) GDPR165. These problems of

effective review raise accountability concerns, which always require significant human control over the 

outcomes of the system166. 

Finally, the legal effects of the epistemic conditions of the system extend to the obligation to state the 

reasons on which decisions are based. The ETIAS clearly illustrates this general problem of algorithmic 

legal decision systems that incorporate certain levels of complexity. The legal relevance of that obligation 

in administrative action [Article 41(2)(c) CFREU] makes it a critical point of the system. 

In this regard, Article 38(2)(c) of the ETIAS Regulation only requires that decisions refusing travel 

authorisation include «a statement of the grounds for refusal of the travel authorisation indicating the 

applicable grounds from those listed in Article 37(1) and (2) enabling the applicant to lodge an appeal». 

In other words, if authorisation is refused because of an unacceptable level of risk, it would be sufficient 

to indicate the threat dimension in which this score is observed (security, illegal immigration or high 

epidemic risk). This is corroborated by the form adopted for notifying refusals, cancellations or 

revocations of travel authorisations, which is highly structured with a checklist of reasons for the 

decision167. While it is true that the form incorporates an open field for the statement of «relevant facts 

and additional reasoning underlying the decision», this is presented as a distinctly secondary or additional 

possibility, not as part of the main argument of an administrative decision. In this logic, there is a 

confusion between a category of reason and the reason for its application. This is similar to the difference 

between the enunciation of a legal precept and the explanation of the reasons for its application to the 

case. The quantitative formality of the system also leads to a formalisation of motivation.  

These conditions for stating reasons in the ETIAS are at odds with the way in which the CJEU has 

interpreted the requirements for administrative reasoning in cases of adverse decisions affecting a 

person168. For the Court, the obligation to state the reasons for the act implies that the decision-making 

body «must disclose in a clear and unequivocal fashion the reasoning followed by the institution which 

adopted the measure», which in turn requires «to identify the actual and specific reasons» underlying it. 

This has the dual purpose of enabling the affected person to challenge the measure and of allowing an 

effective judicial control of legality. More specifically, the Court’s doctrine on the motivation of visa 

refusals is relevant, as the way in which the Visa Code regulates the obligation to state reasons is similar 

to that of the ETIAS169: a checklist is provided that allows the administrative body to mark the reason

for refusal from among those set out in the Regulation together with an open field for adding «remarks» 

[Article 32(1) with reference to Annex VI of the Visa Code]. In the Court’s view, a statement of reasons 

which allows the affected person to respond to the decision requires that he/she «must be able to 

ascertain the specific ground for refusal underlying that decision», which makes it mandatory for the body 

to indicate in the «Remarks» section of the form that specific ground as well as «the essence of the 

reasons» for that objection170. The case concerns the requirement to state reasons for a fully analogical 

decision. The need for legal «narrative» will be even greater where, as in the ETIAS, the decision is based 

on automated processing. 

In the Judgment Council v Nadiany Bamba, the Court gives two further warnings that have a direct 

bearing on the case of the ETIAS171. On the one hand, the obligation to provide reasons for the decision 

becomes more important when the person concerned is not afforded the opportunity to be heard prior 

to the adoption of an initial decision. Recall that the ETIAS algorithm generates an automated decision 

after the application without any possibility of a hearing. The human review of refusals also takes place 

without any hearing of the person concerned. Consequently, the obligation to provide reasons in real and 

specific terms should be read more strictly. 

On the other hand, the Court connects the need to state the reasons for the adverse decision with the 

circumstances of the case and the context in which the decision takes place. Such a reading excludes the 

possibility of standardised arguments (not adapted to the circumstances of the case). In addition, the 

reasons given to justify a measure detrimental to a person are sufficient if the measure was taken in a 

context known to that person and which allows him or her to understand the scope of the measures. In 

a pre-entry procedure (strictly digital), articulated through automated data processing and interoperated 

with other devices (for multiple purposes), the context is a virtual ecosystem that is difficult to assimilate 

in its complexity. The communication of administrative bodies should provide elements of understanding 

of this context and its operational logic, rather than reproducing its opacity. 

However, one should not indulge in hollow legal idealism: to state substantive reasons is a difficult task 

in algorithmic systems that, like the ETIAS, incorporate a wide variety of data and deal with complex 

factors. As Brouwer appreciates in the case of the VIS, it can be very difficult or even impossible to trace 

the source of information that leads to a rejection or expulsion decision172. Even if the variables with the 

most significant weight in a single decision could be identified, the probabilistic rationale of the profiling

7. Concluding remarks 

Considering the regulatory problem of algorithmic technologies, not only as a target of regulation but as 

a tool of law itself, Penasa calls for a «technologically immersive law» as opposed to a «law merely driven 

by technology»174. The ETIAS Regulation is a case of the latter. Its profiling engine extends the legal 

problems inherent to actuarial devices in terms of their classificatory and correlational logic. Moreover, 

its complexity and automated configuration fully affect the regulation of administrative action. 

The formal architecture of the predictive model is at the heart of the Regulation. Its operational 

requirements condition the legal status of the persons concerned. Effective remedies are modulated by 

the priority the legislator gives to the algorithmic model as the normative core for decision-making. 

Paradoxically, the definition of the algorithm itself is omitted from the regulation, entrusting it to a 

process of successive definitions through non-legislative acts and, ultimately, through a formally 

unqualified Frontex decision. 

The Regulations of the EU’s large-scale IT systems, including the ETIAS, and their interoperability 

delineate a regulatory style marked by its cumulative complexity and technological solutionism. They are 

not, however, regulations whose object is a technological product, but incorporate technologies to 

structure the exercise of public powers. As such, the subject is confronted with a networked 

administrative structure shared between National authorities and European bodies and Agencies, where 

the administrative decision is the result of a technologically mediated flow of data. 

As in the case of the interoperability Regulations, the ETIAS one incorporates safeguards addressing the 

requirements of procedural fairness and the processing of personal data. But these safeguards undergo 

an anamorphosis effect when viewed through the algorithmic nature of the Regulations. In the case of 

the ETIAS, this distorting effect conditions the legal architecture of the model by removing central 

aspects of technological regulation from the legislative product. The profiling engine and the algorithm 

behind it are ultimately understood as purely technical, and not political, in nature, even though their

normative impact seems obvious. This externalisation of legislative responsibility produces what might 

be called «defensive technological regulation»175. 

The algorithmic priority that runs through the ETIAS substantially alters the contradictory nature of the 

administrative procedure, the requirements of human supervision of automated praxis, the meaning of 

the obligation for authorities to state their reasons for acting the way they do, and the effective 

possibilities of appeal. 

The need for efficiency in the management of international mobility, together with the securitisation of 

migration policies, are conducive to this regulatory-technological experimentation in the European 

context. The administrative law of border management, already problematic from the point of view of 

the effectiveness of legal guarantees, is profoundly affected by this new regulatory paradigm. In short, 

the commitment to technological management is producing regulations that enable the creation of vast 

networked administrative infrastructures and set up new forms of exercising public powers.  

This development is taking place without compensating for the inadequate legal framework to preserve 

the principle of legality; nor is it apparent that the legal status of the persons affected in the face of a 

public power technologically reassembled is being effectively strengthened.
